text: When using time-in-zone heart rate analysis, approximately 96% of total training time in elite endurance athletes is spent in zone 1 (low intensity), with only ~3% in zone 2 and ~1% in zone 3.

When using session-goal classification, the training intensity distribution shifts to approximately 87% low-intensity sessions, 11% zone 2 sessions, and 2.4% zone 3 sessions, appearing less polarized than time-in-zone analysis.

Different heart-rate-based training quantification methods (time-in-zone vs session-goal) produce significantly different training intensity distributions from the same training data, making cross-study comparisons unreliable without conversion factors.

To convert from time-in-zone to session-goal training distribution, multiply low-intensity proportion by ~0.9 and high-intensity proportion by ~3.0; reverse conversions use factors of ~1.1 and ~0.33 respectively.

Session-goal classification inflates the apparent proportion of high-intensity training by approximately 3-fold compared to time-in-zone analysis, because sessions containing any high-intensity work are classified entirely as high-intensity.

Elite endurance athletes with VO2max of ~76 mL/kg/min follow a highly polarized training distribution where the vast majority of training volume is performed at low intensity regardless of quantification method.
specific_value: VO2max 76 ± 7 mL/kg/min, 86-96% low intensity depending on method
